mnemonics.title: MDP Components
mnemonics.content: S A R P Gamma - Think 'SARPG' or 'SAR Pe Gamma' as in SAR (Search and Rescue) with Gamma rays. Remember: States, Actions, Rewards, Probabilities (transitions), Gamma (discount)

mnemonics.title: Bellman Equation
mnemonics.content: V(s) = R + γ × Future - Value = Reward + Gamma times Future Value. The equation says 'what you get now plus what you'll eventually get, discounted'.

mnemonics.title: Value vs Q-Function
mnemonics.content: V = Value of State, Q = Quality of Action. V(s) is 'how good is here?', Q(s,a) is 'how good is taking action a here?'

acronyms.usage: Mathematical framework for sequential decision-making with stochastic transitions
Learning paradigm where agent interacts with environment using MDP framework
Expected cumulative reward starting from state s
Expected cumulative reward starting from state s and taking action a
Weights immediate vs future rewards; 0 ≤ γ < 1
Mapping from states to actions (or action probabilities)
Cumulative discounted reward from time t onwards

visualTips.visualization: Draw a cycle: Agent → Action → Environment → New State + Reward → Agent. This shows the feedback loop: s → a → (P,R) → s' → a' → ... This is the core interaction pattern.
Imagine a tree branching from current state. V(s) is the sum of all reward branches, with distant branches getting smaller (discounted). Like looking at a tree where leaves farther away matter less.
Think of V(s) as 'this state's value = best immediate reward + discounted value of where you end up'. Each step builds on the next - like ripples in water propagating outward.
In a grid world, draw arrows showing which direction to go from each cell. A deterministic policy is single arrow per cell; stochastic policy has arrows with probabilities.
γ = 0: only care about next step (single line); γ → 1: care about entire future (long chain). Visualize γ as a 'future lens' - higher γ makes distant rewards more visible/important.

analogies.analogy: Think of MDP like navigating a city with a GPS. States = intersections, Actions = turn left/right/straight, Rewards = time saved/delayed, Transition = traffic randomness. Your policy is the navigation strategy.
γ represents patience. Low γ = impatient person who only wants immediate rewards (like spending now). High γ = patient person who plans for future (like saving for retirement).
V(s) is like the high score you'd expect if you start at that point in a video game. Q(s,a) is the score if you also choose a specific power-up at that point.
Policy π is your game strategy - whether to always attack (deterministic) or sometimes defend based on probability (stochastic). The optimal policy is the best overall strategy.

analogies.analody: Bellman equation is like a prophecy that tells you the value of your current situation based on future outcomes. 'The value of now equals the reward now plus the discounted value of what comes next.'
